chapters.title: Hidden Downside Is a Dangerous Kind of Expertise

summaryBullets: Looking for hidden downside before trusting confident systems or experts creates clearer judgment.
Some models look strong only because the damage is delayed or externalized.
This matters because clarity improves when invisible downside is made visible early.
A common mistake is judging a system by smooth short term performance alone.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the upside is obvious while the downside is transferred elsewhere.
A practical starting move is to trace where the downside lands, ask who pays when things go wrong, and review long term rather than only near term performance.
keyTakeaways: Looking for hidden downside before trusting confident systems or experts creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: trace where the downside lands
ask who pays when things go wrong
review long term rather than only near term performance
avoid upside that hides ruin
